event: Martyrdom of an Early Christian Woman
symbols: the wooden post or stake — the instrument of execution, evoking the cross of Christ and the cost of discipleship
the chains and shackles binding the woman — captivity and persecution endured for the sake of faith, the bonds that cannot bind the spirit
the flowing red garment — the blood of martyrdom, the crimson of sacrifice, and the robe of witness (Revelation 7:14)
the inscription 'NAZARAEA' on the post — a follower of the Nazarene (Christ), the name by which early Christians were known and condemned (Acts 24:5)
the sea and rocky shore — the edge of the known world, the boundary between earthly suffering and eternal deliverance
the woman's upward and outward gaze — her eyes fixed not on her executioners but on the unseen glory, looking to the heavenly reward
description: A Pre-Raphaelite painting depicting a young woman with long, flowing red-gold hair, bound to a tall wooden post on a rocky seashore. She wears a voluminous crimson-red gown that billows dramatically in the wind. Her wrists and ankles are shackled with iron chains. A small inscription reading 'NAZARAEA' is affixed to the top of the post. Her expression is composed and resolute, her gaze directed outward with a look of defiant calm. The background features a turquoise sea, a rocky coastline with cliffs, and a warm sky with hints of flame-like clouds.
theological_description: This painting depicts the martyrdom of an early Christian woman identified by the inscription 'NAZARAEA' — a term derived from 'Nazarene,' the designation used for followers of Jesus of Nazareth. In Acts 24:5, the prosecutor Tertullus accuses Paul of being 'a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes,' and this label became a mark both of derision and of identity for the early Church. The bound woman represents the countless unnamed Christians who suffered persecution in the Roman Empire, fulfilling Christ's warning: 'They will lay their hands on you and persecute you... and you will be brought before kings and governors for my name's sake' (Luke 21:12). Her steadfast composure recalls the proto-martyr Stephen, who gazed into heaven even as he was stoned (Acts 7:55-56), and the many women martyrs of the early Church — Perpetua, Felicity, Blandina — whose courage astonished their persecutors. The red garment is rich with scriptural resonance: Revelation 7:14 describes the redeemed as those who 'have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb,' and Revelation 6:9-11 portrays the souls of the martyrs beneath the altar, given white robes and told to wait. The chains that bind her body cannot imprison her spirit, for as Paul wrote from his own chains, 'the word of God is not bound' (2 Timothy 2:9). Evelyn De Morgan's title 'The Martyr' invites the viewer to contemplate the cost of bearing Christ's name in a hostile world — a witness (the Greek martyria) sealed in blood.

scripture_refs.reason: The sect of the Nazarenes — the inscription 'NAZARAEA' identifies the woman as a follower of Jesus the Nazarene, the label used against early Christians
Christ's prophecy that His followers would be persecuted and brought before authorities for His name's sake — the direct context for this martyrdom
The souls of the martyrs under the altar, slain for the word of God and their testimony — the heavenly perspective on this earthly suffering
Those who have come through the great tribulation and washed their robes in the blood of the Lamb — the red garment as symbol of martyrial witness
I am bound with chains as a criminal, but the word of God is not bound — Paul's testimony of faith persisting through physical captivity
Stephen gazing into heaven during his martyrdom — the archetype of the composed, heaven-fixed gaze of the Christian martyr
